cards.front: What is a comment in C++?
cards.back: A comment is a programmer-readable note inserted directly into the source code. Comments are ignored by the compiler and are for the programmer's use only.

cards.text: A single-line comment begins with {{c1:://}} and tells the compiler to ignore everything from that symbol to the end of the line.
A multi-line comment begins with {{c1::/*}} and ends with {{c2::*/}}. Everything between the symbols is ignored.

cards.question: Which lines are comments in this code?
cards.answer: Lines 1, 2 (after the code), and lines 3-4 are all comments
cards.explanation: Single-line comments start with // and multi-line comments are enclosed in /* */. Comments can appear on their own line or after code on the same line.

cards.question: What's wrong with this comment?
cards.answer: Multi-line comments cannot be nested in C++
cards.explanation: When the compiler encounters the first */, it assumes the comment block is complete, even if you intended it to be a nested comment. This can cause unexpected syntax errors.

cards.concept: When to use comments in your code
cards.explanation: At the library, program, or function level, use comments to describe what the code does. Inside the library, program, or function, use comments to describe how the code is going to accomplish its goal.
examples: // Bad: x = x + 1; // Add 1 to x (obvious from code)

cards.text: Comments should be written in a way that makes sense to someone who has {{c1::no idea what the code does}}. Assume the person reading the comments {{c2::understands C++ syntax}}.

cards.front: Should you comment obvious code like x = 5;?
cards.back: No. Programmers often have to read through other people's code. If the code is obvious, don't comment it. Comments should add value by explaining why something is done, not just restating what the code does.

cards.front: What is one common use of comments during development?
cards.back: Commenting out code (replacing it with a comment) to temporarily exclude it from being compiled. This is useful for debugging or testing alternative implementations.

cards.concept: Why you should avoid leaving commented-out code in production
cards.explanation:
Commented-out code makes your codebase harder to maintain and understand:
  • Future readers don't know why it's there
  • They can't tell if it's safe to delete
  • It clutters the code
  • Version control systems (like git) already preserve old code
examples: // int oldCalculation = x * 2; // Why is this here?
Use git to access old code instead
